coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of biocompatible and highly photoluminescent nitrogen doped carbon dots from lime: Analytical applications and optimization using response surface methodology
coredata.dc:creator: Barati, Ali
Shamsipur, Mojtaba
Arkan, Elham
Hosseinzadeh, Leila
Abdollahi, Hamid
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a facile hydrothermal treatment of lime juice to prepare biocompatible nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) in the presence of ammonium bicarbonate as a nitrogen source has been presented. The resulting N-CQDs exhibited excitation and pH independent emission behavior; with the quantum yield (QY) up to 40%, which was several times greater than the corresponding value for CQDs with no added nitrogen source. The N-CQDs were applied as a fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ ions with a detection limit of 14nM. Moreover, the cellular uptake and cytotoxicity of N-CQDs at different concentration ranges from 0.0 to 0.8mg/ml were investigated by using PC12 cells as a model system. Response surface methodology was used for optimization and systematic investigation of the main variables that influence the QY, including reaction time, reaction temperature, and ammonium bicarbonate weight.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Cytotoxicity assay
Response surface methodology
Hg(II) detection
Nitrogen doped

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesize of carbon quantum dots from fruits
Effect of reaction time, temperature and NH4HCO3 weight on quantum yield of N-CQDs
Synthesis and characterization of the N-CQDs
Cellular uptake and cytotoxicity assay of N-CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) The main effects of reaction temperature (X1), reaction time (X2), and ammonium bicarbonate weight (X3) and b) their binary interactions on the QY f N-CQDs prepared from lime.
3D response surface plot of quantum yield versus a) reaction time and temperature, b) weight of ammonium bicarbonate and temperature, c) weight of ammonium bicarbonate and reaction time.

a) TEM, b) XRD, c) FTIR spectra, d) typical PL spectra excited at different excitation wavelengths and UV–vis absorption, e) XPS spectrum and f) XPS high resolution scan of the C1s region of N-CQDs prepared from lime.

Dependency of PL spectra of N-CQDs obtained from a) orange and b) melon on the pH value of the solution. Insets show change in the position of PL spectra maximum and PL intensity at different pH solutions.

a) PL quenching of N-CQDs upon addition of different Hg2+ concentrations in aqueous solution (from top to bottom: 0, 0.02, 0.06, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1, 3, 5, 7, 10μM. λex=340nm). b) The dependence of I/I0 on Hg2+ concentrations from 0 to 10μM. Inset is a linear region from 0 to 0.1μM. c) and d) The PL ratios of the N-CQD aqueous solution in the presence and absence of various metal ions (5μM) and typical anions (50μM). I and I0 are the PL intensities of N-CQDs at 443nm in the presence and absence of Hg2+, respectively.

a) The intracellular fluorescence of N-CQDs in PC12 cells. Inset shows the cells under bright field and blue light excitation after incubating in N-CQDs (0.2mg/ml) culture solution. b) The effect of N-CQDs on PC12 cell viability. The cell viability was determined by MTT assay. Data are average of three separate experiments.
Design matrix for three variables (actual and coded levels) with the observed and predicted values of QY.
The sum of squares and p-values for the regression coefficients of the reduced cubic model with all quadratic terms.
ANOVA results for the reduced cubic model.
QY of N-CQDs obtained from different fruits.
QY and synthesis method of CQDs prepared from different natural and waste materials.

row.entry: Hydrothermal treatment with the assistance of concentrated sulfuric and nitric acids
Heating with the assistance of concentrated sulfuric acid

head.ce:title: Synthesis of biocompatible and highly photoluminescent nitrogen doped carbon dots from lime: Analytical applications and optimization using response surface methodology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Institute for Advanced Studies in Basic Sciences, Zanjan, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Advanced Studies in Basic Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nano Drug Delivery Research Center Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Novel Drug Delivery Research Center, Faculty of Pharmacy, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a facile hydrothermal treatment of lime juice to prepare biocompatible nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) in the presence of ammonium bicarbonate as a nitrogen source has been presented. The resulting N-CQDs exhibited excitation and pH independent emission behavior; with the quantum yield (QY) up to 40%, which was several times greater than the corresponding value for CQDs with no added nitrogen source. The N-CQDs were applied as a fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ ions with a detection limit of 14nM. Moreover, the cellular uptake and cytotoxicity of N-CQDs at different concentration ranges from 0.0 to 0.8mg/ml were investigated by using PC12 cells as a model system. Response surface methodology was used for optimization and systematic investigation of the main variables that influence the QY, including reaction time, reaction temperature, and ammonium bicarbonate weight.

ce:section.ce:para: An emerging new class of nano-carbonaceous materials with photoluminescence properties, later called carbon quantum dots (CQDs), has been receiving growing research interest in a wide range of technologies since they were discovered [1]. Compared to traditional semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes, CQDs are superior in terms of low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, high stability and strong resistance to photodegradation [2]. Considering these advantages, CQDs are nominated as favorite substitutes for conventional semiconductor quantum dots that are based on toxic metallic elements.

Until now, tremendous effort has been spent on developing synthesis methods for various types of CQDs. Different physical or chemical methods such as laser irradiation [3], electrochemical oxidation [4], ultrasonic treatment [5], microwave irradiation [6] and hydrothermal treatment [7] are common approaches for the preparation of CQDs. However, there are some distinct limitations to some of these synthetic methods such as the use of large amounts of strong acids, expensive equipment required and low quantum yield (QY) of the prepared CQDs. Nevertheless, considerable research efforts have been focused on using the hydrothermal treatment because of the simplicity and the versatile chemistry of the method [8,9]. In general, hydrothermal treatment has been shown to turn out as a suitable technique for the preparation of a variety of carbon materials from different carbon sources such as carbohydrates, organic molecules and even from waste biomass [10].

Recently, doping of CQDs with heteroatoms such as nitrogen have been actively pursued and considered as the most promising way to improve fluorescence QY of CQDs as high as those of most conventional semiconductor QDs. For example, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CQDs were produced through a one-step hydrothermal treatment with fluorescence QY of 73% using citric acid as carbon source and l-cysteine as both of nitrogen and sulfur sources [11]. Yang et al. reported highly fluorescent CQDs with QY as high as 80% using citric acid and ethylenediamine [12]. Jiang et al. proposed hydrothermal treatment of sodium citrate in the presence of ammonium bicarbonate to synthesize CQDs with high QY of 68% [13].

On the other hand, several reports have explored the preparation of economical CQDs using natural bio-precursors like orange juice [14], pomelo peel [15], banana juice [16], grass [17], hair fiber [18], and coffee grounds [19,24–26]. However, the prepared CQDs from these raw materials suffer more or less from their low fluorescence QY.

Herein, simple hydrothermal treatment of lime and some other fruits in the presence of ammonium bicarbonate as a nitrogen source has been used to prepare nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs), in order to improve the fluorescence QY of prepared CQDs from these raw materials. Biocompatibility of N-CQDs prepared from lime has been checked using PC12 cells as a model system. In addition, the reaction time, reaction temperature and, ammonium bicarbonate weight were found to have serious effects on the QY of prepared N-CQDs. Response surface methodology (RSM) was carried out in order to optimize the variables as well as systematic investigation of their effects on the QY value.

All fruits were purchased from the local market. NH4HCO3 was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. All other reagents and chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Double deionized water was used throughout all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesize of carbon quantum dots from fruits
ce:section.ce:para: The one-step hydrothermal treatment was used to prepare all N-CQDs as follows. First, the fruits were cut into small pieces and were put in a juicer machine to obtain their crude juices. The crude juices were centrifuged at 6000rpm for 15min and the obtained supernatant was passed through a filter paper with slow filtration speed. Afterward, 10ml of filtrate juice and a suitable amount of NH4HCO3 were transferred into a teflon-lined stainless steel laboratory autoclave and heated in an oven at a constant temperature of 180°C for a period of 7h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting dark solution was centrifuged 10,000rpm for 30min to remove weight precipitate and agglomerated particles to remove large particles. Finally, the diluted solution was filtered through 0.2μm microporous membranes and diluted to further usage.

Experimentally, relative fluorescence QY of all prepared CQDs were determined at an excitation wavelength of 330nm using the following equation:
Q x
=
Q r
I x
I r
A r
A x
η x 2
η r 2
where r and x refer to the standard reference and the sample of interest respectively, Q is the QY, I is the integrated emission spectra, η is the refractive index of the solvent and, and A is the absorbance. The absorbance was kept below 0.1. Fluorescein in 0.1M NaOH with the already known QY of 0.79 was selected as standard reference.

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of N-CQDs were recorded over the range from 500 to 4000cm−1 on a Bruker infrared spectrophotometer. UV–vis spectra were obtained with an Agilent 8453 Diode Array Spectrophotometer and the fluorescence studies were carried out using a Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was obtained by a Zeiss EM10C microscope. XPS analysis was measured with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, 8025-BesTec twin anode XR3E2 X-ray source system). Fluorescence microscopy images were captured using a fluorescent microscope (Olympus IX71; Japan) with the blue light as the excitation source.

Rat pheochromocytoma-derived cell line PC12 was obtained from Pasteur Institute (Tehran, Iran). The PC12 cell line is widely used as a model for dopaminergic neurons because it possesses intracellular substrates for the synthesis, metabolism and transportation of dopamine (DA). This makes PC12 cells useful as a model system for neuroprotection study [32]. The cells were maintained at 37°C in 5% CO2 humidified incubator. Cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) with 10% (v/v) heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum, 100U/ml penicillin and 100mg/ml streptomycin.

Cellular toxicity of N-CQDs was analyzed in PC12 cells using the methyl thiazol tetrazolium bromide (MTT) method. Cells were cultured in a 96-well microplate at a density of 7000 cells/well and in a volume of 200μl. The cells were treated with different doses of N-CQD solutions in wells. At appropriated time intervals, the medium was removed and replaced by 200μl of 0.5mg/ml of MTT in growth medium and then the plates were transferred to a 37°C incubator for 2–4h. Then, the medium was removed, and the purple formazan crystals were dissolved in DMSO (200μ/well). Absorbance was determined on an ELISA plate reader (Biotek, H1M) with a test wavelength of 570nm and a reference wavelength of 630nm to obtain sample signal (OD570–OD630).

For evaluation of C-dot uptake, PC12 cells were cultured on a 6 well cell culture plate for 24h to achieve approximately 80% confluence. A concentrated aqueous C-dot solution (0–0.8mg/ml) was added to designated wells. After incubation for 24h the culture medium containing N-CQDs was discarded. Then cells were lysed with 1% Triton X-100. The fluorescence was measured at an excitation wavelength of 340nm and an emission wavelength of 450nm using a fluorescence microplate reader (BioTek, H1M, USA). For fluorescence microscopy images, the cells were treated with the N-CQDs at the concentration of 0.2mg/ml in 200μl of media and incubated for 5h. Prior to the imaging experiment, the cells were washed three times with PBS buffer. Live-cell-imaging was done under blue light excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reaction time, temperature and NH4HCO3 weight on quantum yield of N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: RSM is a collection of some mathematical and statistical technique to define and represent an empirical relationship between a desired response and independent variables which effectively influence the response as a two or three dimensional hyper surface [20]. The important variables in our experiment were found to be the reaction temperature (X1), reaction time (X2), and NH4HCO3 weight (X3). In order to systematically investigate these variables and explore their main effects as well as their probable interactions on the QY, in the region of investigation, RSM was used. The relative fluorescence QY of all prepared CQDs was determined at an excitation wavelength of 340nm by integrating emission spectra in the region 370–600nm using fluorescein as standard reference (in 0.1M NaOH solution, QY=79%).

Keeping in mind that shorter reaction times and lower temperatures are more favored during the hydrothermal treatment, some trial runs were carried out to define the region of the variables. The lower and upper limits for time (3 and 7h), temperature (120 and 180°C) and, NH4HCO3 weight (0.6 and 1.8g) were selected to design the experiments. 18 experiments according to a face-centered central composite design (F-CCD), including four replicates of the central point, were performed. The corresponding experimental matrix, including the real and coded value of the variables along with the observed QY is presented in Table 1 .

Analysis of the data with various models including linear, quadratic, and cubic models and their subsequent analysis of variances (ANOVA) showed that the reduced cubic model results in an appropriate empirical model to represent the relationship between the variables and observed QY. The results of ANOVA for the reduced cubic model with all quadratic terms are shown in Table 2 . The significance of each regression term was determined by p-value. With the confidence level of 95%, if the absolute probability of coefficient p-value was lower than 0.05, the corresponding term will have a significant effect on the response. The results clearly show that the three main effects (X1, X2, and X3 2), the binary interaction of temperature and NH4HCO3 weight (X1X3), as well as three cubic interactions (X1X2X3, X1 2X2, and X1 2X3) are the terms with the largest effect and therefore will be included in the final model. By considering confidence limits and applying the stepwise strategy for selection of model terms, the following regression equation was obtained.

The statistics ANOVA result of this model is presented in Table 3 . The lack of fit F-value of 0.37 indicates that the lack of fit is not significant relative to the pure error. The R-squared of 0.9916 shows a good fitness of data to the empirical model. Furthermore, the prediction R-squared of 0.9639 is in reasonable agreement with the R-squared and shows a high predictive power of the model. Finally, the observed QYs were best predicted by applying the obtained model (last column of Table 1).

The main effects of temperature, reaction time, and weight of ammonium bicarbonate as well as their binary interactions are graphically presented in Fig. 1a. The main effect for each variable was achieved by changing its value from the low level to the high level and calculating the response where the other two variables held at their central level. For the binary interactions the same process for the first variable was carried out, but at two levels (+1 and −1) of the second variable. It was apparent that all variables show positive effects on the QY. Increasing temperature and reaction time result in a linear gradual increase in QY but in the case of ammonium bicarbonate weight, QY reaches a maximum value with a quadratic effect. However the overall result indicates the temperature as the most effective variable on the QY. The binary interaction between variables is shown in Fig. 1b. Only the interaction of temperature and the ammonium bicarbonate weight was found to be significant. At the lowest level of temperature, ammonium bicarbonate has a relatively low influence on the response, but it turns to be much more effective at the high level of temperature. The curves in other binary interactions are practically parallel, which means that the effect of the first variable is not affected by the level used for the second variable.

The obtained model can be plotted as three-dimensional surfaces representing the response as a function of two variables with one variable kept at a constant value (i.e., central point) and varying the other two within the considered experimental range. Fig. 2a shows the QY of N-CQDs at 1.2g of ammonium bicarbonate as a function of reaction time and temperature. According to this plot a relatively high temperature along with a long reaction time is necessary to prepare N-CQDs with a high QY. Fig. 2b indicates the effect of temperature and ammonium bicarbonate weight at 5h reaction time. The significant interaction between temperature and ammonium bicarbonate is clearly observable in this plot. At 140°C, ammonium bicarbonate shows a small effect on the QY while at 180°C its effect becomes as significant as the temperature effect. Finally, Fig. 2c represents the response at 180°C as a function of reaction time and ammonium bicarbonate weight. In general, we can conclude that the temperature shows the most influence on the QY.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, lime juice was used as a natural carbon source to synthesize cost-effective and water-soluble N-CQDs through one-step hydrothermal method. The optimum values for the reaction time (7h), temperature (180°C) and, ammonium bicarbonate (1.8g) were employed according to the RSM results. The same experimental conditions were employed to synthesize N-CQDs from some other fruits including orange, melon, watermelon, apple, grape and pomegranate to compare their fluorescence properties with those prepared from lime.

Since, fruits contain different constituents such as glucose, fructose, sucrose, citric acid and ascorbic acid, the possible hydrothermal mechanism route for the formation of CQDs from these raw materials is most likely similar to that of carbohydrates. Although a large number of papers on hydrothermal reaction of carbohydrate have been reported, a clear scheme of the formation mechanism and the final material structures has not been reported. This is mainly because of the formation of multitude intermediates from the carbohydrate during the reaction [10].

Nevertheless, based on the vast information reported on this topic, the possible mechanism could be considered as follows. In the first step, due to the high values of the ionic product of H+ and OH− under the hydrothermal conditions, reactions like hydrolysis, dehydration and decomposition of different carbohydrates could take place. For example, the dehydration of glucose can result in different soluble products, such as furfural-like compounds, organic acids, aldehydes and, phenols [21]. Afterward, the monomers like glucose, fructose and/or their decomposition products undergo polymerization or condensation reactions, which lead to the formation of soluble polymeric products. This step probably proceeds in a manner induced by intermolecular dehydration and/or aldol condensation routes. The next step is the aromatization of the polymers which could be resulted from the keto–enol tautomerism of the dehydrated species or intramolecular dehydration [22]. According to the LaMer model for the nucleation, growth mechanism of the carbonaceous materials in the hydrothermal method [23], when the concentration of aromatic clusters in the aqueous solution reaches a critical amount, the nucleation process takes place. Finally, a core–shell structure with a hydrophobic core and a hydrophilic shell that is less dehydrated and contains a large number of reactive oxygen functional groups would result.

The TEM image of N-CQDs prepared from lime (Fig. 3a) shows near spherical nanoparticles with the average diameter of about 7.6±2.7nm. The XRD pattern of this nanomaterial (Fig. 3b) displays a broad peak centered at about 2θ=27°. Considering the interlayer spacing in graphite (0.33nm), these N-CQDs show a graphitic nature with highly disordered carbon atoms [24].

The existence of functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs was studied using FTIR spectra. The FTIR spectra corresponding to the N-CQDs prepared from the lime is shown in Fig. 3c. Peaks at 3200–3550cm−1 and 2800–2900cm−1 are attributed to the OH and CH stretching vibrations, respectively. In addition, stretching vibration of the NH could appear at 3000–3300cm−1. Peaks at about 1600 and 1400cm−1 indicated the presence of CO and CC, respectively. The band at around 1100cm−1 presents the existence of CO (hydroxyl, ester, or ether) groups. These functional groups are almost common in N-CQDs prepared from all other fruits (Fig. S1) and indicate their similar chemical nature. Generally, the existence of these functional groups represents excellent water solubility of these nano-materials without further surface modification.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 3d) of N-CQDs from lime shows a peak at 332nm. N-CQDs from other carbon sources display almost similar UV–vis absorption properties (Fig. S2) with a sharp peak at around 270–280nm and a shoulder at about 300nm. According to the previous studies, these peaks could be attributed to π–π* and n–π* transitions of CC and CO bonds [25]. Fig. 3d presents the normal emission spectra of N-CQDs from lime at the excitation wavelengths ranging from 340 to 420nm. As can be seen, the maximum emission of CQDs from lime appears at 443nm, which is nearly constant at different excitation wavelengths. On the other hand, the emission spectra of CQDs prepared from all other fruits show a typical excitation-dependent property (Fig. S2).

The elementary composition of CQDs prepared from lime in the absence and presence of NH4HCO3 was confirmed by elemental analysis to determine carbon, hydrogen, and nitrogen contents. The results for CQDs in the absence of NH4HCO3 are: C 60.2wt.%; N 2.9wt.%; H 4.7wt.% and O (calculated) 32.2wt.% and for CQDs in the presence of NH4HCO3 are: C 36.8wt.%; N 11.3wt.%; H 4.5wt.% and O (calculated) 47.4wt.%. These observations indicate the incorporation of nitrogen atoms into the CQDs in the presence of NH4HCO3. The surface composition and elemental analysis for the resultant N-CQDs were further characterized by the XPS technique. The three peaks at 285, 400, and 532eV shown in the XPS spectrum of these nanoparticles (Fig. 3e) can be attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The XPS results indicate that these nanoparticles are mainly composed of C, O, and N. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 3f) shows three peaks at 284.4, 286.8, and 288.3eV, which are attributed to CC/CC, CN, and CN/CO, respectively [15].

The QY of N-CQDs prepared from different fruits under optimal synthesis conditions are listed in Table 4 . As can be seen, the highest QY is related to the N-CQDs obtained from lime and orange. To confirm the effect of NH4HCO3 on the QY of prepared CQDs, we further synthesized CQDs under identical synthesis conditions but without NH4HCO3. The resultant CQDs from lime and orange exhibited the QY of 12% and 7%, respectively, and those prepared from other fruits had a relatively weak QY (less than 3%). We also found that CQDs synthesized in ammonia solution (as nitrogen source) had lower QYs than those prepared in the presence of NH4HCO3.
Table 5 shows the QY value of CQDs prepared from different natural and waste materials. As can be seen all CQDs even those doped with nitrogen and/or sulfur have QY lower the 40%.

To study the fluorescence stability of N-CQDs under different conditions, changes in the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs at different pH values and under continuous irradiation of UV light were investigated. The fluorescence intensity of all N-CQD solutions remained almost stable under continuous exposure to UV light up to 3h. The fluorescence study of N-CQDs at various phosphate buffer solutions with pH between 3 and 11, showed that the fluorescence intensity generally increases as pH value increases. Nevertheless, negligible changes (less than 20%) in the fluorescence intensity were observed at pH between 4.0 and 9.0. Interestingly, with the exception of N-CQDs obtained from lime, emission peaks of N-CQDs prepared from all other fruits showed a gradual blue shift (10–20nm) with increase in the pH value of the solution. As an example, changes in the intensity and position of the emission spectra of N-CQDs obtained from orange and melon with the solution pH are presented in Fig. 4 . As can be seen, the wavelength shifts show a good linear relationship with the pH value of the solution.

The existence of many oxygen functional groups on the surface of the CQDs may cause the strong interaction of these nanomaterials with metal ions. Different metal ions including Fe3+ [47], Cu2+ [17] and, Hg2+ [13,15,48] have been reported to effectively quench the fluorescence of CQDs. To evaluate the potential of N-CQDs obtained from lime in analytical applications, their fluorescence intensity in the presence of different metal ions and various anions including were studied. Fig. 5c and d show the effect of these ions on the fluorescence intensity. In agreement with the previous reports, N-CQDs showed high optical selectivity towards Hg2+, while other metal ions and anions have no significant effect on the fluorescence intensity up to this concentration levels. According to the previous reports [13,15,48], the high selectivity toward Hg2+ could be attributed to the strong affinity of Hg2+ toward carboxylic group on the N-CQDs surface compare to other metal ions. This possibly makes N-CQDs close to each other, which accelerates the non-radiative recombination of the excitons through an effective energy or electron transfer process, leading to significant fluorescence quenching. Interaction of Hg2+ ions with N-CQDs was further investigated with UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. A blue shift in the UV–Vis absorption spectra with the addition of Hg2+, indicated that Hg2+ could change the electronic structure of N-CQDs (Fig. S3).

In order to investigate the sensitivity of the fluorescent sensor towards Hg2+, different concentrations of Hg2+ in the range from 0 to 10μM were added into the N-CQDs solution. As shown in Fig. 5a, a continued decrease in the fluorescence intensity with the addition of Hg2+ concentration can be observed. Fig. 5b presents the dependence of I/I 0 on the concentration of Hg2+ where I and I 0 are the fluorescence intensities at 443nm in the presence and absence of Hg2+, respectively. The fluorescence response to Hg2+ concentrations was well fitted to a linear model with the linear correlation coefficient of 0.9866 over the concentration range of 0–0.1μM (inset of Fig. 5b). The limit of detection was found to be 14 nM based on a 3δ/slope, where δ is the standard deviation of the blank signal. The high stability constant of Hg2+ with carboxylate and hydroxyl groups on the surface of N-CQDs can describe the high selectivity of this fluorescent sensor toward Hg2+ ions [13].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake and cytotoxicity assay of N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Since traditional QDs contain toxic heavy metals, their application has raised health and environmental concerns. However, due to the excellent photoluminescence properties and low toxicity of CQDs, they can be an attractive candidate for bioimaging applications. In fact, a literature search shows that CQDs are biocompatible and have been widely used as fluorescence imaging agents in various in vitro and in vivo optical imaging experiments.

Herein, we investigate the cellular uptake and cytotoxicity of N-CQDs obtained from lime at different concentrations range from 0.0 to 0.8mg/ml using PC12 as a model system. The cytotoxicity study was performed with a conventional MTT based assay that relies on the color change of MTT by mitochondrial succinate dehydrogenase [49,50]. As shown in Fig. 6a the intracellular fluorescence of N-CQDs increased dose dependently in PC12 cells. In addition, no cytotoxicity was observed after 24h treatment with N-CQDs (Fig. 6b). It must be noted that the concentrations used here are higher than the usual concentrations required for cell labeling studies.

Fluorescence microscopy image of cells under bright field and blue light excitation after incubating in N-CQDs (0.2mg/ml) culture solution is shown as inset in Fig. 6a. These results clearly show that these N-CQDs can be used as nontoxic labels for cellular and in vivo imaging applications.

In summary, we have reported a simple route to synthesize water soluble and high fluorescent N-CQDs through hydrothermal treatment of raw materials. Due to the introduction of nitrogen atoms, the QYs of N-CQDs prepared from all fruits are significantly elevated. However, N-CQDs from lime showed the highest QY up to 40%. The fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs was remarkably stable over a broad pH range and exposure to UV irradiation. The results of cellular uptake and cytotoxicity assays clearly show that N-CQDs can be used as nontoxic labels for cellular and in vivo imaging applications.

The effect of the affective variables, including reaction time (3–7h), reaction temperature (140–180 °C) and, ammonium bicarbonate weight (0.6–1.8g) on QY was investigated using RSM. According to the results reaction temperature was found to be the most significant variable that influenced the QY. Higher reaction temperature and reaction time as well as high level of ammonium bicarbonate were suitable to prepare N-CQDs with higher QY. The only significant interaction was found between reaction temperature and the ammonium bicarbonate weight, which indicated the higher influence of ammonium bicarbonate on the QY at a higher level of temperature.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2014.11.035.
